sentences.text: Can you just-- just cut a minute.
He--
Yeah.
My name is Teofil Modelski.
My name is Teofil Modelski.
The same name was at birth that I've just said.
M-O-D-E-L-S-K-I.
Model, Modelski.
The 28th of December, 1923, in Poznan Poland, Western side of Poland.
At present, I've only just passed my birthday 20-- 73 years old.
I begin 73 years.
I was Polish.
Oh, religion, Roman Catholic.
Same as I was born.
At the time, Poznan was very industrial time-- town, with about 250,000 citizens before the war.
I'm not sure how-- the amount reflects now.
We lived in Poznan center, and then, during holiday time, we moved about 24 kilometers from Poznan to Brunow, where we had a summer holiday house.
Was a four-bedroom flat, where-- which we usually went winter time, otherwise I had to go to school in Poznan.
And we were very comfortable.
We had no television, at this time, neither-- only radio.
I had my mother, my father, six-- five brothers, plus sister.
My-- my father was Zygmunt Modelski.
My mother was Stanislawa Modelska.
My oldest brother was Zygmunt, then came Izydor, Mieczyslaw, which we called Michael, then Teofil, that's me, Jerzy, that's my twin brother, Barbara, my sister, and the youngest brother, Stanislaw.
Very comfortable.
And we lived very happily.
My father was ex-army major.
He was retired, forced retirement for various political reasons, probably.
And my mother was teacher from profession.
Regarding my father?
There was a change in Poland in 1926.
The main leader of our country, Jozef Pilsudski, has made an outbreak and took dictator, in which he removed all the officers which were against his coup.
It had a certain effect, but at that time, I was-- I was very young, so I didn't realize it.
But later on, my father found himself a job in bank, where he worked.
And we did see no change because he had his pension, plus whatever he earned in bank.
Good standard of living, yes.
We had another home, which was built from-- from beginning.
It wasn't ready.
We had-- our parents had to build it through-- through a construction company.
And there we used to go every time holiday time.
That means from June to-- to September.
Which was very nice surrounding, around-- near the woodland and so on.
My leisure time, I was-- I was still a student.
I started going just before the war.
I started going to grammar school.
And the-- the usual boy fun.
Through the forest, and playing with other boys of my age.
I was a rather naughty boy.
We used to always have a fight with my twin brother, who always stayed with me.
Yes.
We-- I joined the scout movement, which was very good to me, because we had various trips with the scout movement.
And we learned a lot of things to our benefit.
We used to do certain trips, where we played various games and so on.
Therefore, with other boys of similar age, we managed to get together a lot better.
In what way?
Oh, my father was a army major, and therefore, we moved-- we didn't have many friends to come with, because we had a big family to move anywhere.
It wasn't very easy.
So once or twice a year, some friends came, a grammar school teacher, and so on, where my father-- he was my father's best friend.
And that's the only time we've seen anybody.
There were no differences.
There were-- they felt like everybody else.
My father even spoke to a German farmer who-- who lived next to where we had our holiday house.
And he-- he liked to come and conversate with him, because he spoke German, and that way he could just remember his school days, when he was learning German.
Well, there weren't many Jewish community in Western Poznan.
There were more in the east of Poznan, but I wasn't aware of any at that time.
No, I had not heard of any anti-semitism.
Although, I-- I read something in the paper once.
But that was the-- through the German propaganda, which they tried to instill hatred in our country.

Well, Something about the Jewish community being-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] trying to buy various things for various reasons, and cheating the farmers, and so on-- which, in effect, I didn't realize, because I wasn't old enough to understand all this.
No, I wasn't.
I wasn't aware then.
I was more aware near the outbreak of war.
When the German propaganda used to shout that they want the passage through the corridor, Danzig, and so on.
Then I realized, when the Polish army was mobilized and so on, that something is going to happen.
Otherwise, I heard nothing.
My parents were very, very upset.
And naturally, my father wanted to join the Polish army, as well, if necessary to fight for the country.
And it was at that time, when the war broke out, a bit-- before the war broke out, just a few days, we moved-- I, my twin brother, moved to Warsaw in order to join the Polish forces.
But the war was that quick.
On the first day of the war, we were returning from our holiday house to Poznan.
And then we'd seen aeroplanes flying over the-- over us, and we've seen Polish planes trying to knock them down.
Then we realized the war is on.
Well we moved to Poznan, because that was the first stage, when we seen aeroplanes going-- doing, you know, all these bombing and so on.
That's the only thing.
We were coming back to Poznan, yes.
Being on holiday, yes.
Well, we stayed in our house.
We lived on the fourth floor in the house.
There was danger that any bombs coming down may just hit us first.
The only thing we've seen was the aeroplanes throwing bombs over Poznan.
The following day, we-- the planes move further into-- inside Poland.
And all we just wait-- been waiting for, whatever, can happen.
And we have-- I haven't seen any-- any aeroplanes, because from Poznan, when the war started, I moved with my father and the younger brother, my twin brother, to Warsaw to join the Polish forces.
So we were all the time moving forwards whilst the Germans were moving toward Warsaw.
No, we have no encounter.
We-- we went right to Warsaw.
It took us about four days to drive on a bicycle, to ride on a bicycle to-- to Warsaw.
My twin brother, Jerzy, and father.
So three of us went.
Well, we-- we came to the Polish army.
And my father was enrolled.
And we were going to be enrolled as well as kitchen assistants, because I have only 15-year-old, to help in the kitchen.
But soon the Warsaw capitulated and we had to move backwards to Poznan.
When we went back to Poznan, we-- we stayed for a few more weeks the German occupied Poland.
And then, one night, came a German official who said my parents didn't live in Poznan any longer, they were from eastern Poland.
And they-- they asked us to be evacuated.
They said to my father that my mother can stay with the children, but the father must move.
My mother said, no, we go together with our father, because I cannot support the family on my own.
And we moved to where they moved us.
That means they moved us first to Chenstochov, where we stayed at a-- well, it was a church community, where they gave us food and supplied us with living accommodation.
And that's-- that's about it.
Later on, I've tried to move to Hungary with my brother--
I had no encounter.
I had encounter with some soldiers when I was trying to get some milk, which was shortage of.
They didn't know allow big queues.
And they-- one day, they kicked me and said, you go away from there, and don't stand anymore in the queues to get the food.
Well, they-- they kicked me on the bottom once, and that's about it.
And I moved right away.
I didn't see much there, because I didn't stay long after the Germans came.
Well, we-- we tried to-- my two older brothers moved to south Poland in order to go through the Hungarian border, in order to join the Polish forces.
Later on, we received a letter that they passed the border, and they were happy to be in the Polish army.
Then I, with my twin brother and older brother Zygmunt, tried to move as well to south Poland.
But we weren't very fortunate.
We were caught on the border, on the Hungarian border, near the town Bukowsko.
And after interrogations--
We were-- it was in the evening, about 5 o'clock.
Ground, it was thick frost.
It was February.
And we were moving, going, and then German-- German had their patrols on, and they stopped us, asking where are we going?
I try to explain, with my brothers, that we were looking for some-- in order to buy some food in the village, because there was shortage of food.
And they didn't accept this and locked us up at the prison.
Oh, they-- they were very hostile.
And they-- they tried to tell us by beating, tell us you wanted to join the Polish legionaries, [INAUDIBLE] in Hungary.
Speak the truth.
And I said, we-- but we only went here to look for some food, to bring to Poznan, to where my parents lived, in Krakow.
Because they were moved to Krakow later on.
But they didn't believe us and locked us up in prison.
Well-- well, very poor.
I couldn't eat at the beginning.
The food they gave, it was a slice of bread and very watery soup.
First day, I didn't-- didn't manage to eat anything.
But the next day, I felt hungry and I tried to eat.
It was in Sanok, the prison was.
And-- and they brought us three times for interview from the prison, in order to-- to get from us-- to speak the truth what we were trying to do.
Yes.
Each three-- three of us went, and each was interviewed separately.
And every time we were beaten in order to get the confession.
We knew that if we confessed that we were trying to join the Polish forces, they would shoot us, because they did so in many other prisons.
All those who confessed they wanted to join the police forces.
They are three-- there were three Gestapo men which had the rubber truncheons and beat us anywhere they could.
One day, I-- I-- I fainted.
Luckily enough they stopped when I fainted.
But that's the-- the only way they did, by beating.
It didn't-- didn't have any effect, except I was frightened.
I tried to avoid any beating.
One day, I remember, from fright, I even made a mess in my trousers.
And it smelled so much that the Gestapo stopped beating us, because they couldn't stand the smell.
I didn't-- I didn't confess to the crime they wanted from me, to know that I was going to join the Polish forces.
And that made a difference, because we survived.
They also kept to the same, which we agreed what we were going to tell them.
And none of us broke down.
Six months.
They moved us to Tarnow and then to Auschwitz.
About 20 mile-- 20 kilometers.
Well, they moved us because that's where they moved every transport which they were taking from various prisons in Poland.
And there-- from there, they sent further on.
In Tarnow, it was just a matter of a month I stayed. there.
It was no different.
It was more-- more organized prison than Sanok, which was little village.
And I've seen prisoners there, but the life was as bearable as possible.
They not where-- there-- I wasn't interviewed, anything.
They all just-- all-- I was just waiting for the next stage, which was going to Auschwitz.
I didn't know.
I-- I had no idea where-- where we were going and what they're going to do with us.
But I knew they were going to move us somewhere.
By what they called [INAUDIBLE], big lorries.
They put-- chained us up and took us by lorries.
About 40 people-- 20 people in one lorry.
Well, the-- in the lorry wasn't-- not so bad.
They moved us to train.
From the lorries, they moved us to train and loaded us into train, from which we went to Auschwitz.
Well, it was to short journey, so I didn't feel anything except the change, that's all, which makes a difference, always, in a stable life.
How long did it-- the journey last?
About an hour, I think.
They were also taken together with us, with me.
We reached Auschwtiz, where-- where dressed and had our names given and-- and so on.
I came to Auschwitz on the 30th of August, in the summertime, in the morning.
Yeah.
Into Auschwitz, where I've been-- tattoo-- a tattoo written.
No.
I had the-- I seen the prisoners when they stood and looked at the-- the railway line, how we-- how we arrived and so on.
But I had no idea.
They looked very miserable to me.
They had striped clothing on, and it reminded me of prisoners which I've seen on-- on television.
Well, they opened the train, couple of wagons.
And they started shouting, 'Alles out!
Alles out!' And they started beating us in order to make sure we moved quickly.
And then when we were in the Auschwitz camp, they-- we had seen the Lagerkommandant, [?
Hoess, ?] who told us, don't forget you're not coming to a pension-- holiday camp.
You coming to do the concentration camp of Auschwitz.
And after that, he-- he started asking, are there any priests or Jews?
And when a few of them, about 10 of them, came out of there, they took them to the penalty company, which most likely, I doubt they survived.
In the main camp, Auschwitz camp number one.
They were a big accommodation, big room, which people lied on the floor first, because we had no beds.
Later on, they-- so they made some beds, three-- three tier beds, where we-- we slept on a straw mattress.
Well, we all-- well, I stayed with my twin brother, my older brother stayed in different block, where he-- where he was living separate.
First of all, in the morning, you had a gong giving you time 6 o'clock to get up.
And the kapos came into the room with sticks.
And whoever was slowing getting up, they moved-- beat them up to get ready.
Had a quick wash in cold water.
And then an appel, where-- a roll up, they call it in English.
And a roll up where we were counted and then moved to work.

At the beginning, the work was very hard, unloading bricks or digging trenches, which, at the beginning, was not so bad, but later on, I felt, with the amount of food I am consuming, that I have no strength left to carry on with the-- with the speed.
We had once, coffee in the morning, watery coffee, then soup, beetroot soup, or whatever watery soup they gave us.
And then, in the evening, we had a slice of bread.
That's-- that's about all.
Well, I had to consume as much as I can.
And later on, I tried to see if I could get anywhere something to-- to compensate for the lack of food.
Well, it was a matter of getting better job, because on that kind of job, where I had unloading bricks or digging trenches, I knew I will not survive.
And I had an information given that there is a good job going on.
I knew the-- the-- what they call Arbeitsdienst, in German, who gave the work.
He used to live-- we've been-- he's been in the same prison as I was in Sanok.
And he knew me.
So I ask him if I can get-- there is a vacancy at the rabbit feeding company, breeding company.
And he told me, well, I know you.
I can give you that job with your brother.
And that's how I got that job, which was a lot easier, because sometimes I could get some food what the rabbits had.
And it was warm on the roof, which made me easier to survive.
How long did you continue doing that job with the rabbits?
I was doing the job for the first-- well, I-- they moved us from Auschwitz to another kommando outside Auschwitz, where we were living in a Harmeze Schule, special school.
And there, we-- we started living all together.
But they also opened a farm house there, chicken farm.
And there was a fishery farm for-- for crop fish.
And that's where all the people were moved into one place.
The people from fishing camp, they were moved to another place, where was someone was evacuated.
It was Harmensee, that was the village name.
And there-- there was a big house, used to be former palace of [?
Zwilling ?], [?
Zwilling ?] man, he had a big palace, where we lived at the beginning.
But later on, in about half a year later, they employed the women from Birkneau to work on the farm, and we were employed as a subsidiary force for the supply of various items to the Auschwitz.
In Auschwitz, it was very, very hard.
It was-- every time you had to look over your shoulder that nobody will beat you.
The worst-- the worst thing was lack of food.
I would have survived the beating, but the lack of food made it worse.
The German stated that, on this food, you will survive three months unless you get somewhere else food.
People who survived were mainly those who tried to manage to steal some food in order to survive.
Usually when the kommandos were out for work, they tried to look where they can pinch something, either a potato or beet or carrot and-- and eat it.
Or, usually, when the kommandos came back from work, there used to be many people who couldn't survive the day.
And the block managers did not report their death to the kitchen officials in the first few days, so that they can collect their food.
And if you have friends there, they can always give you a helping hand in food.
I was-- I was very good at certain times, but later on, I felt very ill.
I had scratched my foot on a-- on a very hard shoe, and this turned down into phlegmon, which means in-- I had to go to hospital for treatment.
I managed to get an operation done on my leg, in order to remove all the bad blood.
And that, I survived and came back to the block.
About what?
My weight was very, very much down.
But I still was very healthy and managed to survive whatever punishment I received.
Well, sometimes, if you-- if you stood for a while in-- to hide yourself from rain, if somebody see you, you-- you receive beating.
Or if you tried to rest for a bit, you had to also watch that kapo doesn't come along to give you a beating for it.
These are the things which usually worried me.
The cruelty was there.
I've seen-- I've seen the people in the penalty company being beaten.
And they had hard work doing-- and less food than we had.
And this was the worst part for me, when seeing people being thrown down the floor, pulled out gold teeth, and so on.
These were something I had never seen in my life.
Well, I don't know.
I feel a bit awkward.
It's different than writing.
